items.text: Chapter Seven
Life Cycle of Tuta absoluta
Generation Duration, Number of Generations, and Optimal Reproductive Conditions
Introduction
Tuta absoluta undergoes complete metamorphosis, and its life cycle therefore consists of four principal successive stages:
items.prepared_text: [[prepared]]Tuta absoluta undergoes [[term:complete-metamorphosis]]complete metamorphosis[[/term]], and its life cycle therefore consists of four principal successive stages:

items.text: The cycle begins when the mated female lays eggs on the tomato plant. The eggs then hatch, and the larvae enter plant tissues and feed within leaves, growing points, stems, and fruits. After completing the fourth larval instar, the larva stops feeding and transforms into a pupa, from which an adult moth emerges that is capable of mating and producing a new generation.
items.prepared_text: [[prepared]]The cycle begins when the mated female lays eggs on the tomato plant. The eggs then hatch, and the larvae enter plant tissues and feed within leaves, growing points, stems, and fruits. After completing the fourth larval instar, the larva stops feeding and transforms into a pupa, from which an adult moth emerges that is capable of mating and producing a new generation.

items.text: The life cycle of Tuta absoluta is characterized by three principal features:
items.prepared_text: [[prepared]]The life cycle of Tuta absoluta is characterized by three principal features:

items: Short generation duration under suitable warm conditions.
Ability to produce numerous overlapping generations during the year.
Continued reproduction without obligatory diapause as long as food and suitable temperatures are available.
prepared_items: [[prepared]]Short generation duration under suitable warm conditions.
[[prepared]]Ability to produce numerous overlapping generations during the year.
[[prepared]]Continued reproduction without obligatory [[term:diapause]]diapause[[/term]] as long as food and suitable temperatures are available.

items.text: For this reason, the same field may simultaneously contain:
items.prepared_text: [[prepared]]For this reason, the same field may simultaneously contain:

items.text: This overlap makes a single control treatment insufficient because each stage differs in its location and susceptibility to control measures.
items.prepared_text: [[prepared]]This overlap makes a single control treatment insufficient because each stage differs in its location and susceptibility to control measures.

items.text: Beginning of the Life Cycle
The life cycle begins after adult moths emerge from pupae.
items.prepared_text: [[prepared]]The life cycle begins after adult moths emerge from pupae.

items.text: A newly emerged moth requires a short period to:
items.prepared_text: [[prepared]]A newly emerged moth requires a short period to:

items: Complete maturation of its reproductive organs.

items.text: Moths are mainly active during the evening and night, while they hide during the day within the canopy, beneath leaves, and in shaded locations.
items.prepared_text: [[prepared]]Moths are mainly active during the evening and night, while they hide during the day within the canopy, beneath leaves, and in shaded locations.

items.text: The female releases a sex pheromone that attracts males. After mating, the female begins laying eggs following a pre-oviposition period that averages approximately two days under suitable warm conditions and may extend beyond one week under cold conditions.
items.prepared_text: [[prepared]]The female releases a [[term:pheromone-trap]]sex pheromone[[/term]] that attracts males. After mating, the female begins laying eggs following a pre-oviposition period that averages approximately two days under suitable warm conditions and may extend beyond one week under cold conditions.

items.text: The female lays eggs singly or in small groups on the aerial parts of the plant, particularly on young leaves.
items.prepared_text: [[prepared]]The female lays eggs singly or in small groups on the aerial parts of the plant, particularly on young leaves.

items.text: The commonly reported maximum fecundity is approximately:
items.prepared_text: [[prepared]]The commonly reported maximum fecundity is approximately:

items.text: 260 eggs per female
And under some studies and favorable conditions may reach:
items.prepared_text: [[prepared]]And under some studies and favorable conditions may reach:

items.text: 250–300 eggs per female.
However, the actual mean is lower than the maximum and varies according to temperature, host plant, female size, nutrition, and mating history.
items.prepared_text: [[prepared]]However, the actual mean is lower than the maximum and varies according to temperature, [[term:host-plant]]host plant[[/term]], female size, nutrition, and mating history.

items.text: Definition of Developmental Duration and Generation Time
The terms developmental cycle and generation time are sometimes confused, although they differ.
items.prepared_text: [[prepared]]The terms developmental cycle and generation time are sometimes confused, although they differ.

items.text: Development from Egg to Adult
This is the period beginning with oviposition and ending with adult moth emergence from the pupa.
items.prepared_text: [[prepared]]This is the period beginning with oviposition and ending with adult moth emergence from the pupa.

items.text: It includes:
items.prepared_text: [[prepared]]It includes:

items.text: It is referred to as the:
items.prepared_text: [[prepared]]It is referred to as the:

items.text: Egg-to-adult developmental period
Biological Generation Time
This is the period from the laying of an egg in one generation until the resulting female begins laying eggs of the next generation.
items.prepared_text: [[prepared]]This is the period from the laying of an egg in one generation until the resulting female begins laying eggs of the next generation.

items.text: It includes:
items.prepared_text: [[prepared]]It includes:

items.text: Generation time is therefore slightly longer than the period from egg to adult emergence.
items.prepared_text: [[prepared]]Generation time is therefore slightly longer than the period from egg to adult emergence.

items.text: Adult Longevity
The entire adult lifespan is not included in calculating the beginning of the next generation because the female begins laying eggs long before it dies.
items.prepared_text: [[prepared]]The entire adult lifespan is not included in calculating the beginning of the next generation because the female begins laying eggs long before it dies.

items.text: The female may continue laying eggs for several days after the next generation has begun. Generations therefore overlap rather than one complete cohort ending before the next begins.
items.prepared_text: [[prepared]]The female may continue laying eggs for several days after the next generation has begun. Generations therefore overlap rather than one complete cohort ending before the next begins.

items.text: General Sequence of the Life Cycle
Under suitable warm conditions, the cycle can be summarized as follows:
items.prepared_text: [[prepared]]Under suitable warm conditions, the cycle can be summarized as follows:

rows: Complete generation from egg to egg

items.text: These durations are not fixed. The generation may be shortened to approximately 20 days under particular thermal conditions, while it may extend beyond 60 days during winter and cold periods.
items.prepared_text: [[prepared]]These durations are not fixed. The generation may be shortened to approximately 20 days under particular thermal conditions, while it may extend beyond 60 days during winter and cold periods.

items.text: Duration of Development at Different Temperatures
Temperature largely controls the rate of development of all stages of Tuta absoluta.
items.prepared_text: [[prepared]]Temperature largely controls the rate of development of all stages of Tuta absoluta.

items.text: A study conducted under constant temperatures recorded the following durations:
items.prepared_text: [[prepared]]A study conducted under constant temperatures recorded the following durations:

items.text: These results show that increasing temperature within the range tolerated by the insect greatly accelerates development.
items.prepared_text: [[prepared]]These results show that increasing temperature within the range tolerated by the insect greatly accelerates development.

items.text: Development declined from:
items.prepared_text: [[prepared]]Development declined from:

items.text: 115.4 days at 10°C
To:
items.prepared_text: [[prepared]]To:

items.text: 23.5 days at 25°C
And then to:
items.prepared_text: [[prepared]]And then to:

items.text: 18.3 days at 30°C.
However, developmental speed alone does not determine temperature suitability. Development may be completed rapidly at high temperature while survival, fecundity, and hatching success decline.
items.prepared_text: [[prepared]]However, developmental speed alone does not determine temperature suitability. Development may be completed rapidly at high temperature while survival, fecundity, and hatching success decline.

items.text: Actual Generation Time at Different Temperatures
When the pre-oviposition period is added to the developmental period from egg to adult, approximate generation time becomes:
items.prepared_text: [[prepared]]When the pre-oviposition period is added to the developmental period from egg to adult, approximate generation time becomes:

items.text: These values show that the generation can be completed from egg to egg in approximately:
items.prepared_text: [[prepared]]These values show that the generation can be completed from egg to egg in approximately:

items.text: 26 days at 25°C.
At 10°C, it may require more than four months.
items.prepared_text: [[prepared]]At 10°C, it may require more than four months.

items.text: Nevertheless, in the same study, 30°C was not the most favorable temperature for reproduction despite producing the shortest generation time because female fecundity and egg viability declined sharply.
items.prepared_text: [[prepared]]Nevertheless, in the same study, 30°C was not the most favorable temperature for reproduction despite producing the shortest generation time because female fecundity and egg viability declined sharply.

items.text: The Fastest Temperature Is Not Always the Optimum
A distinction must be made between:
items.prepared_text: [[prepared]]A distinction must be made between:

items: The temperature producing the fastest growth.
The temperature producing the highest survival.
The temperature producing the highest fecundity.
The temperature producing the fastest population increase.
prepared_items: [[prepared]]The temperature producing the fastest population increase.

items.text: In one study, the insect completed development from egg to adult in 18.3 days at 30°C compared with 23.5 days at 25°C.
items.prepared_text: [[prepared]]In one study, the insect completed development from egg to adult in 18.3 days at 30°C compared with 23.5 days at 25°C.

items.text: However, at 30°C the study recorded:
items.prepared_text: [[prepared]]However, at 30°C the study recorded:

items: A major reduction in female fecundity.
A severe decline in egg-hatching percentage.
A negative effect on overall survival.

items.text: At 20–25°C, the following occurred together:
items.prepared_text: [[prepared]]At 20–25°C, the following occurred together:

items: High survival of eggs, larvae, and pupae.
High proportion of fertilized eggs capable of hatching.
Better continuity of the next generation.
prepared_items: [[prepared]]High survival of eggs, larvae, and pupae.
[[prepared]]High proportion of fertilized eggs capable of hatching.

items.text: Therefore, 30°C is not an absolute optimum even if developmental duration is shorter at that temperature.
items.prepared_text: [[prepared]]Therefore, 30°C is not an absolute optimum even if developmental duration is shorter at that temperature.

items.text: Optimal Thermal Conditions for Reproduction
The most suitable and stable thermal range for development and reproduction of Tuta absoluta is approximately:
items.prepared_text: [[prepared]]The most suitable and stable thermal range for development and reproduction of Tuta absoluta is approximately:

items.text: Recent demographic models estimated the optimum range for survival of immature stages and female fecundity at approximately:
items.prepared_text: [[prepared]]Recent demographic models estimated the optimum range for survival of immature stages and female fecundity at approximately:

items.text: A temperature of 25°C is highly suitable for generation development. One study recorded at this temperature:
items.prepared_text: [[prepared]]A temperature of 25°C is highly suitable for generation development. One study recorded at this temperature:

items: Mean fecundity: approximately 149 eggs per female.

items.text: Values vary among populations, strains, and cultivars, but most studies indicate that moderately warm temperatures provide the best balance among development, survival, and reproduction.
items.prepared_text: [[prepared]]Values vary among populations, strains, and cultivars, but most studies indicate that moderately warm temperatures provide the best balance among development, survival, and reproduction.

items.text: Effect of Low Temperatures
Cold conditions slow all growth and reproductive processes.
items.prepared_text: [[prepared]]Cold conditions slow all growth and reproductive processes.

items.text: As temperature decreases:
items.prepared_text: [[prepared]]As temperature decreases:

items: The number of annual generations decreases.

items.text: Reduced activity does not mean disappearance of the pest. Some stages can survive during winter, particularly:
items.prepared_text: [[prepared]]Reduced activity does not mean disappearance of the pest. Some stages can survive during winter, particularly:

items: Pupae in soil and crop residues.
Stages occurring on persistent solanaceous plants.

items.text: The life cycle may extend in winter to:
items.prepared_text: [[prepared]]The life cycle may extend in winter to:

items.text: More than 60 days
And may exceed 90 days during very cold periods.
items.prepared_text: [[prepared]]And may exceed 90 days during very cold periods.

items.text: Effect of High Temperatures
High temperature accelerates development up to a certain point, after which heat-stress effects begin to appear.
items.prepared_text: [[prepared]]High temperature accelerates development up to a certain point, after which heat-stress effects begin to appear.

items.text: Continuous extreme heat may cause:
items.prepared_text: [[prepared]]Continuous extreme heat may cause:

items: Increased mortality of newly hatched larvae.
Lower survival of the next generation.

items.text: Air temperature alone does not represent the temperature experienced by the pest. A larva inside a mine occupies a different microclimate, and dense foliage, irrigation, and greenhouses provide milder conditions than the surrounding atmosphere.
items.prepared_text: [[prepared]]Air temperature alone does not represent the temperature experienced by the pest. A larva inside a mine occupies a different microclimate, and dense foliage, irrigation, and greenhouses provide milder conditions than the surrounding atmosphere.

items.text: Infestation may therefore continue during periods of high temperature, especially within:
items.prepared_text: [[prepared]]Infestation may therefore continue during periods of high temperature, especially within:

items.text: Thermal Requirements of the Generation
Insect development depends not only on the number of days, but also on the amount of heat accumulated above the lower developmental threshold.
items.prepared_text: [[prepared]]Insect development depends not only on the number of days, but also on the amount of heat accumulated above the lower developmental threshold.

items.text: The lower thermal threshold for the egg-to-adult cycle has been estimated at approximately:
items.prepared_text: [[prepared]]The lower thermal threshold for the egg-to-adult cycle has been estimated at approximately:

items.text: The heat required to complete development from egg to adult has been estimated at approximately:
items.prepared_text: [[prepared]]The heat required to complete development from egg to adult has been estimated at approximately:

items.text: 416.7 accumulated heat units — Degree-Days.
The upper thermal threshold for development has been estimated at approximately:
items.prepared_text: [[prepared]]The upper thermal threshold for development has been estimated at approximately:

items.text: Daily heat accumulation can be calculated in a simplified form as follows:
items.prepared_text: [[prepared]]Daily heat accumulation can be calculated in a simplified form as follows:

items.text: Daily degree-days = Mean daily temperature − 8
When the mean temperature is above 8°C.
items.prepared_text: [[prepared]]When the mean temperature is above 8°C.

items.text: Example at a Mean Temperature of 25°C
Daily heat units:
items.prepared_text: [[prepared]]Daily heat units:

items.text: 25 − 8 = 17 degree-days.
Theoretical duration required to complete development:
items.prepared_text: [[prepared]]Theoretical duration required to complete development:

items.text: 416.7 ÷ 17 = approximately 24.5 days.
This result is close to the experimentally recorded egg-to-adult duration at 25°C.
items.prepared_text: [[prepared]]This result is close to the experimentally recorded egg-to-adult duration at 25°C.

items.text: The complete pre-oviposition period is not included in the egg-to-adult thermal constant, so generation time from egg to egg is slightly longer.
items.prepared_text: [[prepared]]The complete pre-oviposition period is not included in the egg-to-adult thermal constant, so generation time from egg to egg is slightly longer.

items.text: Absence of Obligatory Diapause
Tuta absoluta larvae do not enter obligatory diapause as long as food is available and conditions permit development.
items.prepared_text: [[prepared]]Tuta absoluta larvae do not enter obligatory diapause as long as food is available and conditions permit development.

items.text: This means that the insect does not require a complete seasonal cessation of growth at a fixed time.
items.prepared_text: [[prepared]]This means that the insect does not require a complete seasonal cessation of growth at a fixed time.

items.text: When the following are available:
items.prepared_text: [[prepared]]When the following are available:

items.text: The stages can continue developing and reproducing during long periods of the year.
items.prepared_text: [[prepared]]The stages can continue developing and reproducing during long periods of the year.

items.text: This is particularly important in:
items.prepared_text: [[prepared]]This is particularly important in:

items: Fields in which residues have not been removed.
Regions where tomato is cultivated year-round.
prepared_items: [[prepared]]Fields in which residues have not been removed.

items.text: Overlapping Generations
Not all Tuta absoluta individuals in a field develop at the same time.
items.prepared_text: [[prepared]]Not all Tuta absoluta individuals in a field develop at the same time.

items.text: Some females may enter the field earlier than others, egg laying continues for several days, and developmental rate varies according to:
items.prepared_text: [[prepared]]Some females may enter the field earlier than others, egg laying continues for several days, and developmental rate varies according to:

items.text: Generations therefore overlap, making it difficult to distinguish first, second, and third generations through field inspection alone.
items.prepared_text: [[prepared]]Generations therefore overlap, making it difficult to distinguish first, second, and third generations through field inspection alone.

items.text: A single plant may simultaneously carry:
items.prepared_text: [[prepared]]A single plant may simultaneously carry:

items: A pupa inside a folded leaf.
An adult moth hidden among leaves.

items.text: This overlap is one of the reasons a continuous management program is required rather than a treatment directed at a single day.
items.prepared_text: [[prepared]]This overlap is one of the reasons a continuous management program is required rather than a treatment directed at a single day.

items.text: Number of Generations per Year
Tuta absoluta is a multivoltine insect:
items.prepared_text: [[prepared]]Tuta absoluta is a multivoltine insect:

items.text: Multivoltine insect
Meaning that it can produce several generations during the year.
items.prepared_text: [[prepared]]Meaning that it can produce several generations during the year.

items.text: Under suitable conditions with continuous availability of the host, it may produce:
items.prepared_text: [[prepared]]Under suitable conditions with continuous availability of the host, it may produce:

items.text: 10–12 generations annually.
Some references estimate:
items.prepared_text: [[prepared]]Some references estimate:

items: Up to 12 generations in greenhouses and continuous production systems.
Fewer generations in cold regions or areas with short growing seasons.
prepared_items: [[prepared]]Up to 12 generations in greenhouses and continuous production systems.
[[prepared]]Fewer generations in cold regions or areas with short growing seasons.

items.text: Recording 12 generations does not mean that every generation lasts a fixed 30.4 days. Summer generations are short, winter generations are longer, and generations overlap.
items.prepared_text: [[prepared]]Recording 12 generations does not mean that every generation lasts a fixed 30.4 days. Summer generations are short, winter generations are longer, and generations overlap.

items.text: Number of Generations in Egypt
The expected number of generations in Egypt varies according to region, temperature, planting season, and calculation method.
items.prepared_text: [[prepared]]The expected number of generations in Egypt varies according to region, temperature, planting season, and calculation method.

items.text: One study based on temperatures recorded during 2012 estimated generation numbers as follows:
items.prepared_text: [[prepared]]One study based on temperatures recorded during 2012 estimated generation numbers as follows:

items.text: These values show that warmer regions can produce more generations during the year.
items.prepared_text: [[prepared]]These values show that warmer regions can produce more generations during the year.

items.text: Other models using different degree-day values predicted:
items.prepared_text: [[prepared]]Other models using different degree-day values predicted:

items.text: 11–13 generations under some Egyptian field conditions.
These estimates do not necessarily conflict directly because they differ in:
items.prepared_text: [[prepared]]These estimates do not necessarily conflict directly because they differ in:

items: Definition of generation beginning and end.
Use of monthly means or daily temperatures.
Inclusion or exclusion of the adult stage.
prepared_items: [[prepared]]Use of monthly means or daily temperatures.
[[prepared]]Inclusion or exclusion of the adult stage.

items.text: There is therefore no single fixed number applicable to every Egyptian governorate and every year.
items.prepared_text: [[prepared]]There is therefore no single fixed number applicable to every Egyptian governorate and every year.

items.text: Number of Generations Within One Tomato Crop
The number of generations occurring within a single crop depends on:
items.prepared_text: [[prepared]]The number of generations occurring within a single crop depends on:

items: Persistence of the field after final harvest.
prepared_items: [[prepared]]Persistence of the field after final harvest.

items.text: In a tomato crop lasting 120 days, with a mean generation time of 25–30 days, it is theoretically possible for:
items.prepared_text: [[prepared]]In a tomato crop lasting 120 days, with a mean generation time of 25–30 days, it is theoretically possible for:

items.text: Four to five generations
To occur.
items.prepared_text: [[prepared]]To occur.

items.text: However, infestation may not begin on transplanting day, and moths may enter midway through the season. The actual number may therefore be lower or more strongly overlapping.
items.prepared_text: [[prepared]]However, infestation may not begin on transplanting day, and moths may enter midway through the season. The actual number may therefore be lower or more strongly overlapping.

items.text: During winter, a generation may require 50–80 days, so only one or two complete generations may occur within the crop. Nevertheless, prolonged crop duration allows larvae and different stages to accumulate.
items.prepared_text: [[prepared]]During winter, a generation may require 50–80 days, so only one or two complete generations may occur within the crop. Nevertheless, prolonged crop duration allows larvae and different stages to accumulate.

items.text: Optimal Humidity
Tuta absoluta succeeds across a wide humidity range, but survival and reproduction are favorable under moderate relative humidity.
items.prepared_text: [[prepared]]Tuta absoluta succeeds across a wide humidity range, but survival and reproduction are favorable under moderate relative humidity.

items.text: The practical suitable range in many studies and laboratory-rearing systems is approximately:
items.prepared_text: [[prepared]]The practical suitable range in many studies and laboratory-rearing systems is approximately:

items.text: 50–70% relative humidity.
Moderate humidity helps:
items.prepared_text: [[prepared]]Moderate humidity helps:

items: Improve survival of newly hatched larvae.

items.text: A study conducted at 20.5°C and 55% relative humidity recorded:
items.prepared_text: [[prepared]]A study conducted at 20.5°C and 55% relative humidity recorded:

items: Greater fecundity than under conditions of 32°C and 40% relative humidity.
prepared_items: [[prepared]]Greater fecundity than under conditions of 32°C and 40% relative humidity.

items.text: Humidity alone does not determine performance because it interacts with temperature.
items.prepared_text: [[prepared]]Humidity alone does not determine performance because it interacts with temperature.

items.text: Reduced survival at low humidity may also result from high temperature rather than desiccation alone.
items.prepared_text: [[prepared]]Reduced survival at low humidity may also result from high temperature rather than desiccation alone.

items.text: Effect of High and Low Humidity
Low Humidity
It may cause:
items.prepared_text: [[prepared]]It may cause:

items: Mortality of exposed newly hatched larvae.

items.text: Very High Humidity
It may cause:
items.prepared_text: [[prepared]]It may cause:

items: Damage to some eggs and pupae.
Reduced soil aeration when combined with water saturation.
prepared_items: [[prepared]]Reduced soil aeration when combined with water saturation.

items.text: Moderate humidity with good ventilation remains more suitable than severe dryness or continuous wetness.
items.prepared_text: [[prepared]]Moderate humidity with good ventilation remains more suitable than severe dryness or continuous wetness.

items.text: Effect of the Host Plant on Generation Duration
Tomato is the most suitable host for the development and reproduction of Tuta absoluta, but developmental rate varies among tomato cultivars.
items.prepared_text: [[prepared]]Tomato is the most suitable host for the development and reproduction of Tuta absoluta, but developmental rate varies among tomato cultivars.

items.text: Generation performance is affected by plant characteristics including:
items.prepared_text: [[prepared]]Generation performance is affected by plant characteristics including:

items: Balance between vegetative and reproductive growth.

items.text: Egg-to-adult developmental time may vary by several days among cultivars.
items.prepared_text: [[prepared]]Egg-to-adult developmental time may vary by several days among cultivars.

items.text: Host quality also affects:
items.prepared_text: [[prepared]]Host quality also affects:

items.text: A short developmental period alone is not sufficient to identify the most suitable host. Generation duration must be studied together with survival and fecundity.
items.prepared_text: [[prepared]]A short developmental period alone is not sufficient to identify the most suitable host. Generation duration must be studied together with survival and fecundity.

items.text: Availability of Young Growth
Females prefer to lay eggs on young leaves and plant parts, and larvae begin feeding in tender tissues that are easier to penetrate.
items.prepared_text: [[prepared]]Females prefer to lay eggs on young leaves and plant parts, and larvae begin feeding in tender tissues that are easier to penetrate.

items.text: Plants with continuous vegetative growth therefore help:
items.prepared_text: [[prepared]]Plants with continuous vegetative growth therefore help:

items: Improve survival of newly hatched larvae.
Maintain movement of larvae among leaves and growing points.
prepared_items: [[prepared]]Maintain movement of larvae among leaves and growing points.

items.text: Excessive nitrogen fertilization may produce tender and dense growth, increasing plant suitability for the pest and providing shaded sites where moths can hide.
items.prepared_text: [[prepared]]Excessive nitrogen fertilization may produce tender and dense growth, increasing plant suitability for the pest and providing shaded sites where moths can hide.

items.text: This does not mean that reducing nitrogen alone controls Tuta, but that balanced fertilization forms part of reducing environmental suitability for infestation.
items.prepared_text: [[prepared]]This does not mean that reducing nitrogen alone controls Tuta, but that balanced fertilization forms part of reducing environmental suitability for infestation.

items.text: Effect of Overlapping Planting Seasons
Overlapping planting seasons are among the most important causes of life-cycle continuity.
items.prepared_text: [[prepared]]Overlapping planting seasons are among the most important causes of life-cycle continuity.

items.text: A single region may simultaneously contain:
items.prepared_text: [[prepared]]A single region may simultaneously contain:

items: Fields in which harvest has ended.
Crop residues that have not been removed.
prepared_items: [[prepared]]Crop residues that have not been removed.

items.text: When an old field completes harvest, moths emerge from it and move to younger fields.
items.prepared_text: [[prepared]]When an old field completes harvest, moths emerge from it and move to younger fields.

items.text: This situation forms a continuous green bridge that allows the pest to complete generations without interruption.
items.prepared_text: [[prepared]]This situation forms a continuous [[term:green-bridge]]green bridge[[/term]] that allows the pest to complete generations without interruption.

items.text: Risk increases when:
items.prepared_text: [[prepared]]Risk increases when:

items: Spraying stops in the old field.
Plants remain alive after the final harvest.
Wind movement is directed toward the new field.
prepared_items: [[prepared]]Plants remain alive after the final harvest.
[[prepared]]Wind movement is directed toward the new field.

items.text: Optimal Conditions for Reproduction
The highest reproductive and population-growth capacity occurs when the following conditions are combined:
items.prepared_text: [[prepared]]The highest reproductive and population-growth capacity occurs when the following conditions are combined:

rows: Overlapping planting seasons throughout the year
Dense, providing shade and a favorable microclimate
Loose soil, residues, and folded leaves
Sufficient numbers of males and females
Absence or weakness of an effective program
Presence of resistance to the groups used
prepared_rows: [[prepared]]Dense, providing shade and a favorable microclimate
[[prepared]]Absence or weakness of an effective program
[[prepared]]Presence of resistance to the groups used

items.text: No single factor alone produces a population explosion. High density results from the combination of suitable temperature, continuous host availability, high fecundity, and weak mortality factors.
items.prepared_text: [[prepared]]No single factor alone produces a population explosion. High density results from the combination of suitable temperature, continuous host availability, high fecundity, and weak mortality factors.

items.text: Capacity for Population Increase
A single female can lay up to 260 eggs, and some females may reach 300 eggs.
items.prepared_text: [[prepared]]A single female can lay up to 260 eggs, and some females may reach 300 eggs.

items.text: Not all eggs develop into adults because losses occur due to:
items.prepared_text: [[prepared]]Not all eggs develop into adults because losses occur due to:

items.text: Even when only a limited proportion of offspring survives, the pest can increase rapidly because of:
items.prepared_text: [[prepared]]Even when only a limited proportion of offspring survives, the pest can increase rapidly because of:

items.text: If a female lays 260 eggs and only 10% reach the adult stage, this can produce 26 adults from one female. If approximately half are females, a new group of females begins the cycle again.
items.prepared_text: [[prepared]]If a female lays 260 eggs and only 10% reach the adult stage, this can produce 26 adults from one female. If approximately half are females, a new group of females begins the cycle again.

items.text: Life Cycle in Summer and Winter
Summer Cycle
It is usually characterized by:
items.prepared_text: [[prepared]]It is usually characterized by:

items: Rapidly increasing risk of fruit infestation.

items.text: The generation may be completed in approximately:
items.prepared_text: [[prepared]]The generation may be completed in approximately:

items.text: 20–30 days
When temperatures are warm but not severely stressful.
items.prepared_text: [[prepared]]When temperatures are warm but not severely stressful.

items.text: Winter Cycle
It is characterized by:
items.prepared_text: [[prepared]]It is characterized by:

items.text: The generation may extend to:
items.prepared_text: [[prepared]]The generation may extend to:

items.text: 60–90 days or more
Depending on severity of cold, greenhouse conditions, and the local microclimate within plants.
items.prepared_text: [[prepared]]Depending on severity of cold, greenhouse conditions, and the local microclimate within plants.

items.text: Slow winter development does not mean absence of risk because larvae may remain inside plants for longer periods, and stages may persist until weather improves.
items.prepared_text: [[prepared]]Slow winter development does not mean absence of risk because larvae may remain inside plants for longer periods, and stages may persist until weather improves.

items.text: Life Cycle in Open Fields and Greenhouses
Open Fields
The cycle is directly affected by:
items.prepared_text: [[prepared]]The cycle is directly affected by:

items.text: The number of generations may decline in regions with cold winters or periods without a tomato crop.
items.prepared_text: [[prepared]]The number of generations may decline in regions with cold winters or periods without a tomato crop.

items.text: Greenhouses
Greenhouses generally provide:
items.prepared_text: [[prepared]]Greenhouses generally provide:

items.text: The pest can therefore complete more generations and may reach approximately 12 generations annually in structures where tomato production continues during most of the year.
items.prepared_text: [[prepared]]The pest can therefore complete more generations and may reach approximately 12 generations annually in structures where tomato production continues during most of the year.

items.text: Applied Importance of Understanding the Life Cycle
Understanding the life cycle allows control measures to be timed rather than applied randomly.
items.prepared_text: [[prepared]]Understanding the life cycle allows control measures to be timed rather than applied randomly.

items.text: Targeting Eggs
This prevents larval emergence and produces the best result when using:

items.text: Targeting Newly Hatched Larvae
L1 and L2 are more susceptible than larger larvae, especially before entering fruits and stems.
items.prepared_text: [[prepared]]L1 and L2 are more susceptible than larger larvae, especially before entering fruits and stems.

items.text: Targeting Moths
Moths are monitored with pheromone traps and reduced through:
items.prepared_text: [[prepared]]Moths are monitored with pheromone traps and reduced through:

items: Prevention of migration from old fields.

items.text: Use of Traps to Monitor Generations
Pheromone traps detect activity of male moths.
items.prepared_text: [[prepared]]Pheromone traps detect activity of male moths.

items.text: An increase in trap catches may indicate:
items.prepared_text: [[prepared]]An increase in trap catches may indicate:

items: Emergence of a new cohort of adults from pupae.
Immigration of moths from a neighboring field.
prepared_items: [[prepared]]Emergence of a new cohort of adults from pupae.
[[prepared]]Immigration of moths from a neighboring field.

items.text: However, the trap alone cannot determine the generation number because generations overlap and moths continue emerging over extended periods.
items.prepared_text: [[prepared]]However, the trap alone cannot determine the generation number because generations overlap and moths continue emerging over extended periods.

items.text: Trap interpretation should be linked with inspection of:
items.prepared_text: [[prepared]]Trap interpretation should be linked with inspection of:

items.text: Comprehensive Life-Cycle Table

rows: Approximately 25.8 days from egg to egg
Lower thermal threshold for the cycle
Absent while food and suitable temperature are available
prepared_rows: [[prepared]]Absent while food and suitable temperature are available

items.text: Chapter Summary
Tuta absoluta passes through four stages: egg, larva, pupa, and adult. The larval stage comprises four successive instars and is responsible for damage.
items.prepared_text: [[prepared]]Tuta absoluta passes through four stages: egg, larva, pupa, and adult. The larval stage comprises four successive instars and is responsible for damage.

items.text: Development from egg to adult requires approximately 23.5 days at 25°C, while generation time from egg until the beginning of oviposition by the next generation is approximately 25.8 days.
items.prepared_text: [[prepared]]Development from egg to adult requires approximately 23.5 days at 25°C, while generation time from egg until the beginning of oviposition by the next generation is approximately 25.8 days.

items.text: Generation duration increases sharply as temperature declines. Egg-to-adult development may require 63.9 days at 15°C and more than 115 days at 10°C.
items.prepared_text: [[prepared]]Generation duration increases sharply as temperature declines. Egg-to-adult development may require 63.9 days at 15°C and more than 115 days at 10°C.

items.text: Increasing temperature to 30°C shortens developmental duration but does not guarantee optimal reproduction because extreme temperatures may reduce survival, fecundity, and egg-hatching percentage.
items.prepared_text: [[prepared]]Increasing temperature to 30°C shortens developmental duration but does not guarantee optimal reproduction because extreme temperatures may reduce survival, fecundity, and egg-hatching percentage.

items.text: The most suitable and stable range for pest development and reproduction is 20–25°C. Optimum conditions for immature-stage survival and female fecundity are estimated at approximately 21–23°C, with moderate relative humidity generally between 50 and 70%.
items.prepared_text: [[prepared]]The most suitable and stable range for pest development and reproduction is 20–25°C. Optimum conditions for immature-stage survival and female fecundity are estimated at approximately 21–23°C, with moderate relative humidity generally between 50 and 70%.

items.text: Under suitable conditions, the pest produces approximately 10–12 generations annually. The number in Egypt may range from approximately seven generations in mild coastal regions to more than nine generations in warm regions, while some models predict higher values when host plants and suitable temperatures remain continuously available.
items.prepared_text: [[prepared]]Under suitable conditions, the pest produces approximately 10–12 generations annually. The number in Egypt may range from approximately seven generations in mild coastal regions to more than nine generations in warm regions, while some models predict higher values when host plants and suitable temperatures remain continuously available.

items.text: Larvae do not enter obligatory diapause as long as food is available. The cycle therefore continues in greenhouses, overlapping crops, and field residues.
items.prepared_text: [[prepared]]Larvae do not enter obligatory diapause as long as food is available. The cycle therefore continues in greenhouses, overlapping crops, and field residues.

items.text: The pest’s severity results from the combination of high fecundity, which may reach 260–300 eggs per female, short generation time, overlapping stages, and multiple annual generations.
items.prepared_text: [[prepared]]The pest’s severity results from the combination of high fecundity, which may reach 260–300 eggs per female, short generation time, overlapping stages, and multiple annual generations.

items.text: Successful integrated management therefore depends on breaking the life cycle at several points, beginning with eggs and young larvae, continuing through pupae in soil and residues, and ending with monitoring adult moths and preventing their movement among fields.
items.prepared_text: [[prepared]]Successful integrated management therefore depends on breaking the life cycle at several points, beginning with eggs and young larvae, continuing through pupae in soil and residues, and ending with monitoring adult moths and preventing their movement among fields.
